function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}

# SpaceX S-1 Space Segment Revenue Breakdown — Detailed Findings

## (1) Launch Services vs. Launch & Development — What's Included

The S-1 draws a clear distinction between the two Space sub-segments:

**Launch Services:**
> "Launch Services for the deployment of payloads to their intended orbits for both commercial and government customers utilizing Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy."

- Derived from **fixed-price contracts ranging from one to five years**
- Revenue recognized **at a point in time** — specifically "upon the launch or deployment of the customer's payload to their intended orbit"
- Rationale: "due to the interchangeability of flight hardware and minimal unique engineering costs"
- Revenue and costs are **deferred** until launch/deployment

**Launch & Development:**
> "Launch and Development for the development of spacecraft and provision of launch and mission services for government agency space programs utilizing Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Starship, and Dragon."

- Derived from **fixed-price contracts ranging from one to fourteen years** (notably longer duration)
- Revenue recognized **over time** using the **cost-to-cost input method**
- Recognition condition: "the Company's performance on the contract creates an asset with no alternative use and the Company has an enforceable right to payment for performance to date"
- Notably includes **Starship and Dragon** — vehicles not mentioned in Launch Services
- Predominantly **government agency** space programs

The MD&A explicitly states the expectation that Launch & Development will grow as a share of Space revenue:
> "We expect Launch and Development to represent a larger portion of our Space revenue as we continue to serve our long-term contracts for our government customers."

The percentage mix table shows Launch & Development rising from 31.8% (2024) → 37.0% (2025) → 46.7% (Q1 2026), confirming this trajectory.

---

## (2) Per-Launch Revenue / Average Revenue per Launch

The S-1 **references** the concept but **does not disclose a specific dollar figure** for average revenue per launch. The only direct mention is:

> "While total Falcon launches increased by 31 from 134 in 2024 to 165 in 2025, Space customer launches and average price per launch remained relatively flat year over year."

One can **derive an implied figure** from the disaggregated revenue table and launch counts, but the filing itself does not state it. Using the disclosed data:

| Period | Launch Services Revenue | Total Falcon Launches | Customer Launches (implied) |
|--------|------------------------|-----------------------|------------------------------|
| 2024 | $2,584M | 134 | Not separately disclosed |
| 2025 | $2,576M | 165 | "remained relatively flat" |

The filing notes that total Falcon launches include internal Starlink deployments, so dividing Launch Services revenue by total Falcon launches would **overstate** the denominator. The S-1 does not provide a clean customer-launch count that would allow precise per-launch calculation.

---

## (3) External Customer Launches vs. Internal Starlink Launches — Separate Disclosure

**Yes, the S-1 explicitly separates these**, though the treatment is asymmetric:

**External customer launches → recognized as Space segment revenue:**
> "Our Space segment revenue only reflects our customer launches and customer activities."

**Internal Starlink launches → NO inter-segment revenue recognized:**
> "For launches of our Starlink satellites, the Company does not recognize any inter-segment revenue, rather those launch costs are capitalized in satellites in Property, plant, and equipment, net."

> "For launches dedicated to deploying our Starlink satellites, we capitalize the associated costs within our Connectivity segment and depreciate them over time, and we do not recognize revenue for those launches in our Space segment."

The filing also explains the operational dynamic that creates period-to-period volatility:
> "When an expected customer payload for a planned launch is not available, we instead use launch capacity for our satellites. As a result, we adjust expected launch payloads frequently, impacting period-to-period financial comparison."

And it quantifies the cost capitalization impact:
> "...the relative increase in Starlink satellite launches from 63 launches in 2023 to 89 launches in 2024, resulting in relatively more of our launch operations and overhead costs capitalized in our Connectivity segment of $99 million."

The S-1 also flags future capacity reallocation to a third internal use:
> "We allocate a significant amount of launch capacity to our Connectivity segment, and expect to allocate a significant amount to our AI segment in the future."

However, the filing does **not** provide a standalone line item showing the **count** of customer launches vs. internal Starlink launches in a given period. ## Implied Revenue-Per-Launch Comparison: SpaceX (S-1) vs. Rocket Lab (FY2025 10-K)

### 1. Rocket Lab — Directly Disclosed Figure

Rocket Lab's FY2025 10-K (filed Feb 26, 2026) **directly discloses** a "revenue per launch" metric with a formal definition:

> "Revenue per launch represents the average transaction price attributable to launch contract performance obligations during the period in which the launch occurs, regardless of whether the revenue is recognized as point-in-time or over-time method of revenue recognition."

| Year | Launch Services Revenue | Electron Launches | **Disclosed Revenue/Launch** | Disclosed Cost/Launch |

| 2025 | $199.0M | 21 | **$8.5M** | $4.8M |
| 2024 | $125.4M | 16 | **$7.8M** | $5.7M |
| 2023 | $71.9M | 10 | **$7.1M** | $7.0M |

Note: Simple division of segment revenue by launch count (e.g., $199.0M ÷ 21 ≈ $9.5M) does **not** equal the disclosed $8.5M because the metric is defined as average *transaction price* attributable to launch performance obligations, not recognized segment revenue divided by launches. Rocket Lab also cautions that "growth rates between launches and total launch service revenue are not perfectly correlated."

### 2. SpaceX — Implied Proxy (Constructed from S-1 Disclosures)

SpaceX's S-1 (filed May 20, 2026) discloses Space segment revenue split into two sub-lines and Falcon launch counts split between customer and internal (Starlink) launches, but **does not disclose a per-launch revenue figure**. The S-1 only states qualitatively: *"Space customer launches and average price per launch remained relatively flat year over year."*

| 2025 | $2,576M | $1,510M | $4,086M | 43 | 122 | 165 |
| 2024 | $2,584M | $1,212M | $3,796M | 45 | 89 | 134 |
| 2023 | $1,964M | $1,593M | $3,557M | 33 | 63 | 96 |

**Implied revenue per customer launch (Launch Services revenue ÷ customer launches)** — the closest analog to Rocket Lab's Launch Services segment:

| Year | Calculation | Implied $/Launch |
|------|------------|------------------|
| 2025 | $2,576M ÷ 43 | **~$59.9M** |
| 2024 | $2,584M ÷ 45 | **~$57.4M** |
| 2023 | $1,964M ÷ 33 | **~$59.5M** |

This proxy is directionally consistent with the S-1's qualitative statement that "average price per launch remained relatively flat" between 2024 ($57.4M) and 2025 ($59.9M) — a ~4% change. (Using total Space revenue ÷ customer launches yields $84.4M→$95.0M, a 12.6% increase that is *not* "relatively flat," confirming that the S-1's "average price per launch" concept most closely maps to the Launch Services sub-line.)

### 3. Directional Comparison

| Metric | Rocket Lab (Electron, 2025) | SpaceX (Falcon, 2025, implied) | Ratio (SpaceX/RKLB) |
|--------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|---------------------|
| Revenue per launch | $8.5M (disclosed) | ~$59.9M (implied) | **~7.0x** |
| Payload to LEO | ~300 kg | ~22,800 kg | ~76x |
| Implied revenue per kg | ~$28,300/kg | ~$2,630/kg | ~0.09x (SpaceX is ~11x cheaper per kg) |
| Booster reusability | Largely expendable | 157 of 165 Falcon 9 launches (95.2%) used flight-proven boosters | — |

### 4. What the Comparison Reveals About Vertical Integration and Reusability

**On absolute revenue per launch:** SpaceX captures roughly **7x** more revenue per customer launch than Rocket Lab. This is driven primarily by vehicle class — Falcon 9 deploys ~76x more mass to orbit per mission than Electron, so SpaceX serves fundamentally different (larger, higher-value) payloads and missions.

**On per-kg economics (the reusability + vertical integration signal):** The more revealing comparison is per-kilogram. SpaceX's implied ~$2,630/kg is roughly **one-eleventh** of Rocket Lab's ~$28,300/kg. This dramatic gap is the economic signature of two structural advantages:

1. **Vehicle reusability:** In 2025, 157 of 165 Falcon 9 launches (95.2%) used flight-proven (previously flown and recovered) first-stage boosters. This collapses the marginal hardware cost per launch toward second-stage + fairing + propellant + operations, rather than a full new vehicle. Rocket Lab's Electron is predominantly expendable, so each launch consumes a complete new vehicle — its disclosed cost per launch ($4.8M in 2025) is ~56% of its revenue per launch, leaving limited margin headroom. SpaceX does not disclose cost per launch, but the 95% booster-reuse rate implies a structurally lower marginal cost ratio.

2. **Vertical integration:** SpaceX manufactures its Merlin engines, avionics, structures, and fairings in-house, capturing the full launch value chain and avoiding supplier margins. Rocket Lab is also vertically integrated (it builds its own Rutherford engines and much of Electron), but at far smaller scale, limiting unit-cost amortization. SpaceX's vertical integration compounds with reusability: in-house recovery, refurbishment, and reflight of boosters/fairings is only economically viable because SpaceX controls the hardware design and manufacturing.

**Directional takeaway:** The comparison shows that reusability and vertical integration do **not** reduce SpaceX's *revenue* per launch (which remains high in absolute terms because the vehicle is large) — rather, they compress the *cost* per launch and per kilogram, allowing SpaceX to pass through lower per-kg pricing to customers while preserving margin. Rocket Lab, operating a smaller expendable vehicle, must charge far more per kilogram to cover its full-vehicle cost structure. The ~7x revenue-per-launch gap understates the competitive divergence; the ~11x per-kg gap is the more economically meaningful figure.

### 5. The Specific Disclosure Missing from the SpaceX S-1

To make this comparison **precise rather than approximate**, the SpaceX S-1 would need to disclose what Rocket Lab's 10-K discloses but SpaceX's S-1 omits:

**Primary missing disclosure — a directly stated, defined "average revenue per launch" (or "average price per launch") KPI:**
- Rocket Lab provides a formally defined metric ("average transaction price attributable to launch contract performance obligations") with explicit dollar values ($8.5M / $7.8M / $7.1M) and a companion cost-per-launch metric ($4.8M / $5.7M / $7.0M).
- SpaceX's S-1 **references** the concept ("average price per launch remained relatively flat") but **does not quantify it** and does not define it as a KPI. The figure must be reverse-engineered from disaggregated revenue and launch counts.

**Secondary missing disclosure — a clean mapping of customer launches to revenue sub-lines:**
- The S-1 discloses total customer launches (43 in 2025) but does **not** split them between Launch Services missions (point-in-time revenue, Falcon 9/Falcon Heavy payload deployment) and Launch & Development missions (over-time revenue, government programs using Falcon 9/Falcon Heavy/Starship/Dragon). Because Launch & Development revenue is recognized over time using cost-to-cost input — not tied 1:1 to a launch event — mixing these into a single customer-launch denominator makes any per-launch calculation inherently imprecise. Rocket Lab avoids this problem by disclosing revenue per launch as a transaction-price metric independent of recognition timing.

**Tertiary missing disclosure — cost per launch:**
- Rocket Lab discloses cost per launch, enabling a direct gross-margin-per-launch comparison. SpaceX's S-1 provides no equivalent, so the margin impact of reusability cannot be quantified from the filing — only inferred from the 95% flight-proven booster rate.

In short: the SpaceX S-1 provides enough data to construct an **approximate** implied revenue-per-launch proxy (~$57–60M for 2023–2025), but it lacks the **directly disclosed, defined average-revenue-per-launch figure** (and the customer-launch-to-sub-line mapping, and cost-per-launch) that Rocket Lab's 10-K provides — which is precisely the disclosure that would convert this directional comparison into a precise one.
